description: Successful publish response MUST contain exactly the five registry-assigned fields (ctx_id, lineage_id, version, created_at, status) and MUST NOT echo content_hash, signature, or any body field. status MUST be 'active' on a fresh publication. The response schema is additionalProperties: false; consumer deserializers MUST NOT rely on additional fields appearing. (0.2.0) One scoped exception: a registry advertising the acdp-registry-receipts profile additionally returns the registry_receipt member — and nothing else (RFC-ACDP-0003 §4, RFC-ACDP-0010 §7); registries not advertising that profile MUST NOT include it, and every forbidden_fields entry below stays forbidden for all registries.
version_note_0_2_0: Under acdp/0.2.0, run this fixture against a non-receipts registry as written. Against an acdp-registry-receipts registry, the expected shape is the five fields plus a registry_receipt member that itself verifies per rcpt-001/RFC-ACDP-0010 §8; its absence from such a registry is the failure pinned by fed-009's missing-receipt case.

input.body_summary: A schema-valid first-version publish request with a verifiable signature. Concrete payload omitted; any pub-001-style well-formed body whose signature verifies is acceptable as input to this fixture.

headers.Location: MUST be the percent-encoded canonical retrieval URL for the assigned ctx_id (e.g. /contexts/acdp%3A%2F%2F<authority>%2F<uuid>)
Location_encoding_rules: The colon (`:`) in the `acdp` scheme delimiter MUST be percent-encoded as `%3A`.

Each forward slash (`/`) inside the `acdp://` URL MUST be percent-encoded as `%2F`. This includes the two slashes immediately following `acdp:` (the `//` authority separator) AND the slash separating authority from UUID.
Hex digits in the percent-encoding MUST be uppercase (`%3A`, `%2F`) per RFC 3986 §2.1's recommendation; lowercase is accepted by RFC 3986 but registries SHOULD emit uppercase for consistency.
The leading `/contexts/` portion of the Location path is NOT itself percent-encoded — only the `ctx_id` payload is.
Result form: `/contexts/acdp%3A%2F%2F<authority>%2F<uuid>` where `<authority>` and `<uuid>` are themselves NOT further encoded (they contain only characters legal in a URI path).

field_constraints.ctx_id: Form: acdp://<authority>/<uuid>; <authority> equals the registry's declared authority.
field_constraints.lineage_id: Form: lin:sha256:<64-lowercase-hex>; deterministically derived per RFC-ACDP-0001 §5.6.
field_constraints.version: Integer == 1 for first-version publication; previous.version + 1 otherwise.
field_constraints.status: MUST be the literal string 'active' on a successful publish response.

rationale: Echoing content_hash from a publish response confers no integrity guarantee — the producer already submitted it and signed it. Reference implementations have included content_hash in publish-response structs, masking shape divergences via test mocks that mirror the same wrong shape. This fixture pins the exact response surface.

scenarios.name: Location header round-trip — consumer reconstructs ctx_id from Location
scenarios.description: After a successful publish, a consumer reads the `Location` response header, percent-decodes the path component after `/contexts/`, and asserts the result equals `body.ctx_id` byte-for-byte. This catches registries that emit a partially-encoded Location (e.g. only the colon encoded but not the slashes), and registries that emit a path-relative URL inconsistent with the body field.

consumer_steps: 3. Percent-decode the remaining path segment using RFC 3986 §2.1 rules.
expected.consumer_behavior: Consumer MUST treat any divergence between (Location-decoded) and `body.ctx_id` as a registry bug and surface it. Acceptable handling: refuse to use the response, raise a schema/integrity error. Consumer MUST NOT silently prefer one over the other.

negative_examples.note: All three of these forms MUST cause the consumer to flag the response as non-conformant. The first two are RFC 3986 violations (slashes in a path segment that already contains the `acdp:` scheme delimiter would re-anchor the path); the third is a server-side identity divergence.
